heading: libro delle Leggi, dicendo, in sostanza: quanti negano che Dio provveda alle cose umane, sono costretti ad ammettere che ciò avviene o
card_summary: This chunk of Zorzi's De Harmonia Mundi (Cantico Secondo, Tono Settimo, chs. 24–25) argues for divine providence against its detractors, drawing on Plato's Laws, Lactantius, Augustine, Habakkuk, Psalms 73 and 44, Jeremiah, Origen, and Paul. Zorzi defends Christ's governance of the Church against objections about evil, false prophets, and the unequal distribution of charisms, using the body-soul analogy. Chapter 25 then presents an elaborate Kabbalistic gematria argument — combining the numerical value of the Hebrew spelling of David's name in Chronicles (with yod), the years of David's life, and the years from his death to Christ — to demonstrate that the oracle of 2 Samuel 7 ("when your days are completed") prophesies Christ, reaching the mystical sum of 1000 years as the full human life-cycle, and extending this through calculations reaching 1035 and 606, the latter equated with the combined gematria of the names Jesus and Mary. The passage concludes with David's own response to the oracle, citing the phrase "this is the law of Adam" (Torah ha-Adam), whose Hebrew final letters sum to 1000, interpreted as the lex hominis fulfilled in Christ.
essay:
# Chunk 83: Divine Providence, Christ's Governance, and Kabbalistic Chronology of the Davidic Oracle

## Overview

Pages 1967–1980 of the *De Harmonia Mundi* span two chapters. Chapter 24 (continuing from the previous chunk) resolves objections to divine providence by defending God's — and specifically Christ's — governance of the cosmos and the Church. Chapter 25 opens a sustained Kabbalistic and numerological exegesis of the Davidic oracle in 2 Samuel 7, arguing that its Hebrew text encodes the precise chronological relationship between David and Christ.

---

## Chapter 24: Against Objectors to Providence

### The Standard Objections

Zorzi opens *in medias res*, continuing an argument drawn from Plato's *Laws* (explicitly cited, note 647): those who deny God's care for human affairs must say either that God *cannot*, *will not*, or *neglects* governance. The rhetorical form is closely parallel to Lactantius (note 646), who provides the analogy of the expert architect who plans not only the construction but the long-term preservation of his building:

> Certo non manca la conoscenza a Colui che sa tutto. Né manca la capacità di governare a Colui che tutto può. Egli non si è rifiutato né ha disdegnato di governare le cose che ha creato, quali che siano, dalle più nobili alle più modeste.

<cite>p. 1967–1968 (Italian translation column)</cite>

The argument moves from the craftsman analogy to a proof that universal governance requires particular care: a doctor, helmsman, military commander, father, and magistrate cannot attend to the whole while ignoring the parts. God's governance of the whole therefore implies governance of each human being.

### The Problem of Evil and the Complaint of the Saints

Zorzi acknowledges that this theodicy faces a sharp counterweight: the manifest prosperity of the wicked and suffering of the just. He marshals the canonical complainants — Habakkuk 1 ("I cry out, suffering violence, and you do not save me?"), Psalm 73 ("my feet nearly slipped…I envied the wicked seeing the prosperity of sinners"), Jeremiah, Augustine, and Jerome — as witnesses that even the greatest heroes in God's kingdom were shaken by this scandal.

His response draws on an Augustinian maxim celebrated in *De quantitate animae*: "Non quaeras, quae supra te sunt" — do not inquire into what is above you. Zorzi links this to Christ's thanksgiving in Matthew 11:25 ("I praise you, Father, because you have hidden these things from the wise and prudent"). The incomprehensibility of divine justice is itself, Zorzi suggests, the *gloria* of God's equitable rule.

The Moses typology from Exodus 33 is then deployed: Moses asks to see God's glory (i.e., his mode of governance); God replies that Moses cannot see his face (the reason behind his acts) but only his back (the effects and results). The "sanctuary" of Psalm 73 — "I will understand their end when I enter the sanctuary of God" — is identified with the eschatological judgment when each receives according to his works.

The interim response is doxological: *"Iustus es Domine, et rectum iudicium tuum."*

### Augustine's Carpenter and the Use of Crooked Tools

A key Augustinian analogy (note 660) vindicates the apparent disorder within divine governance:

> Egli infatti, in virtù di quella sapienza che sa trarre il bene da ogni male, conosce il modo di adattare a un uso retto le cose che paiono storte a vantaggio di quelli che governa, secondo l'esempio proposto da Agostino del falegname, per il quale sono necessari e opportuni strumenti ricurvi e contorti non meno di quelli diritti.

<cite>p. 1803 (Latin/Italian, Cantico Secondo)</cite>

### Objections Specific to Christ's Governance

Zorzi sharpens the theodicy by listing distinctly Christological objections: Why is Christ's redemption rejected by so many? Why does he permit his vineyard to be destroyed by the "singular beast" (the devil, the tyrant of this world, whom Christ boasts of having expelled)? Why did Christ preach in Corazim and Bethsaida rather than Tyre and Sidon, when he himself declared that Tyre and Sidon would have repented? Why are greater charisms distributed unevenly?

The response operates on two registers. First, the body-soul analogy: just as even a well-governed body suffers humoral disturbances (since the body is by nature corruptible), so the Church, composed of *viatores* (pilgrims still combating), cannot avoid some members being "altered" — to expect otherwise would be a *contradictio in adiecto*. Christ nonetheless never ceases to strengthen the weak.

Second, Origen's answer to the Tyre/Sidon question is cited and then improved: Origen said Tyre "would not have persisted," but Zorzi notes this is a weak response. The true answer is the Pauline doctrine of the *plenitudo temporis* (Galatians 4:4): just as Christ came at the fullness of time for the redemption of humanity, so Tyre received its fullness of time when Paul arrived and baptized its citizens (Acts 21). The distribution of charisms follows the same logic as the body's natural physiology — purer blood to the heart, finer spirits to the brain.

> Eodem modo de quibuscumque aliis dicendum est, quia expectavit, et expectat tempora, quae Pater posuit in sua potestate, et quae noscere nostrum non est.

<cite>p. 1806 (Latin column)</cite>

---

## Chapter 25: The Davidic Oracle as Prophecy of Christ — A Kabbalistic Computation

### The Oracle of 2 Samuel 7 / 1 Chronicles

Chapter 25 pivots to a detailed reading of God's promise to David ("when your days are completed and you rest with your fathers, I will raise up your offspring after you, who will come from your bowels"). Zorzi's first move is philological: the Hebrew *mi-me'eka* (מִמֵּעֶיךָ) means "from the bowels/womb," which applies not to the father but to the mother — and thus points to the Virgin birth of Christ, born solely of a woman who was David's daughter and flesh.

The oracle's subsequent verses — "I will establish his kingdom," "he will build a house for my name," "I will make his throne firm forever" — are shown to be inapplicable to Solomon, whose kingdom quickly fractured (Ahijah of Shiloh's promise to Jeroboam is cited). The prophecy therefore refers to Christ, the King of Kings and Lord of Lords.

### Psalm 44 and the Letter Mem

Zorzi introduces Psalm 44 (45), which he reads as an alphabetical acrostic. At the letter *mem* (מ), the verse reads *malkuteka* (מַלְכוּתְךָ) — "your kingdom is the kingdom of all ages" — confirming eternal Davidic-Messianic kingship.

### The Gematria of David's Name and the Millennium

The computational heart of the chapter is a precise Kabbalistic chronology demonstrating that the oracle's phrase "when your days are completed" encodes the millennium as the ideal human life-span:

- From creation to Christ: **3784 years**
- From creation to David's death: **2880 years**
- Therefore from David's death to Christ: **906 years**
- David's lifespan: **70 years** → 906 + 70 = **976**
- The gematria of David's name *as written in Chronicles* (with yod: דָּוִיד = ד+ו+י+ד = 4+6+10+4 = **24**) → 976 + 24 = **1000**

Zorzi notes that David's name is written differently in Kings (without yod: דָּוִד = 14) and in Chronicles (with yod: 24), and insists this discrepancy is not accidental — it conceals the mystery.

> Non ci si sorprenda se si aggiunge qui il numero corrispondente a un nome perché, se qui non fosse nascosto un mistero profondo, Giovanni non avrebbe detto nell'Apocalisse: Chi ha intelligenza calcoli il numero della bestia, che rappresenta il nome di un uomo. Anche i pitagorici professavano di poter pronosticare molte cose sulla base dei numeri corrispondenti ai nomi.

<cite>p. 1808 (Italian column)</cite>

### The Sum of 1035 and the Gematria of the Hebrew Oracle

A second computation extends this to the exact phrase of the oracle in Hebrew: "when your days are completed and you will sleep etc." The sum of all the letters in these Hebrew words is stated to be **1035** — the number of years from David's conception to Christ's death. This incorporates Christ's 33.5 years of life plus the 1.5 years obtained by adding the 9 months of David's gestation and the 9 months of Christ's, making 35 complete years beyond the millennium.

### The Sum of 606 and the Names of Jesus and Maria

A further computation removes one letter from a word in the oracle, leaving a form whose gematria is **606** — and Zorzi identifies 606 as also the combined gematria of the Hebrew names *Yeshu* (ישו) and *Miriam* (מרים):

> Se si toglie la lettera [ו] dalla parola [וישן], rimane [ישן], la cui somma dà 606, che è anche la somma dei valori numerici delle lettere dei nomi di Gesù e di Maria: [ישו מרים], come è evidente per chi sa.

<cite>p. 1810 (Italian column)</cite>

### David's Response: Torah ha-Adam

The chapter closes with David's response to the oracle (2 Sam 7:19): "this is the law of Adam, Lord God" (*zo't torat ha-adam*, זֹאת תּוֹרַת הָאָדָם). Zorzi performs a final gematria on the *final letters* of the Hebrew words in this phrase, a method he identifies as celebrated among Hebrew scholars (*per extremas aut primas literas dictionum*). The terminal letters sum to **1000**. He then reads "the law of the man" as the *lex hominis* fulfilled in Christ, citing 1 Corinthians 15:21: "For as by a man came death, so also by a man comes the resurrection of the dead."

---

## Connections to Zorzi's Larger Project

This chunk exemplifies Zorzi's characteristic fusion of Christian theodicy, Neoplatonic hierarchy, and Kabbalistic computation. The body-soul-Church analogy links the macrocosmic harmony of *De Harmonia Mundi* to the microcosm of ecclesiastical governance. The Kabbalistic chronology (gematria, notarikon of final letters) is deployed as the "secret theology of the Hebrews" (*secretior theologia Hebraeorum*) that confirms Christian revelation. The insistence that Christ is the fulfillment of the Davidic covenant integrates the Christological and cosmological dimensions of the entire work — Christ as the legitimate heir to whom the Father has transferred universal governance is both the Neoplatonic *Nous* descending into matter and the Kabbalistic Messiah encoded in the numerological fabric of Scripture.
